title: User Guide
text:
This guide explains how to use the Shiny app to investigate whether TenantThread’s embargo breach was caused by deliberate leakage or systemic breakdown.
Launch the deployed Shiny app

title: User Guide
text:
Recommended Workflow

Start with Landing Page / Overview.
Use Crisis Timeline to reconstruct when events happened.
Use Agent Network to identify who was involved.
Use Embargo Breach Pathway to trace sensitive information movement.
Use evidence tables to support conclusions.

title: User Guide
text:
Landing Page / Overview
Purpose:
Use this tab to understand the case context, the main investigation question, and the overall structure of the app.
Key controls:
This page is mainly for orientation. Start here before moving into the detailed analytical tabs.
What to look for:
Look for the main crisis themes, the suspected breach context, and the app sections that will help you move from general overview to specific evidence.
How the output contributes evidence:
The overview helps frame the investigation. It explains what the app is trying to test, so later findings from the timeline, network, and pathway views can be interpreted against the same investigation question.

title: User Guide
text:
Crisis Timeline
Purpose:
Use this tab to reconstruct when key crisis events happened and how the situation developed over time.
Key controls:
Use the date range filters to focus on a specific period. Use keyword search to find messages, events, or actors linked to a topic. Use the crisis phase filter to compare activity across different stages of the crisis. Use the range slider to zoom into a narrower part of the timeline when the full period is too broad.
What to look for:
Look for sudden increases in activity, repeated references to sensitive information, and event clusters around the embargo breach. Pay attention to whether key events happen before or after public disclosure.
How the output contributes evidence:
The timeline supports evidence about sequence and timing. It helps users decide whether the breach followed a clear chain of events or whether the crisis emerged from broader communication breakdowns.

title: User Guide
text:
Agent Network
Purpose:
Use this tab to identify who was involved, how agents were connected, and whether specific people or groups appear central to the crisis.
Key controls:
Use the focal agent filter to inspect connections around one selected person or entity. Use keyword search to narrow the network to relevant topics. Use the crisis phase filter to compare how relationships change across the investigation period.
What to look for:
Look for highly connected agents, unusual bridges between groups, repeated contact with high-risk channels, and agents who appear close to sensitive information flows.
How the output contributes evidence:
The network view supports evidence about involvement and communication structure. It can help show whether the breach depends on one central actor, a small group of connected actors, or a wider systemic pattern.

title: User Guide
text:
Embargo Breach Pathway
Purpose:
Use this tab to trace how sensitive information may have moved across agents, channels, and crisis events.
Key controls:
Use the channel risk filter to focus on higher-risk communication channels. Use the anomaly-only filter to show unusual or suspicious records. Use the date range filters and range slider to focus on the likely breach window. Use keyword search to isolate messages or evidence linked to embargoed content. Hide and show evidence tables when you need more space for the pathway diagram or when you need to inspect the supporting records.
What to look for:
Look for paths where sensitive information moves from internal sources to external or public-facing channels. Pay attention to anomalies, high-risk channels, and repeated links between the same agents or events.
How the output contributes evidence:
The pathway view supports evidence about possible information movement. It helps connect agents, channels, and events into an investigative summary that can be checked against the underlying records.

title: User Guide
text:
Evidence Tables
Evidence tables should be used throughout the investigation. They provide the detailed records behind the visual outputs and help users verify claims before drawing conclusions.
Use the hide and show evidence table controls depending on the task. Hide the tables when you need to focus on the visual pattern. Show the tables when you need to inspect message details, timestamps, agents, channels, crisis phases, or anomaly flags.

title: User Guide
text:
Interpretation Note
The causal chain and pathway diagrams are investigative summaries and should be read together with the linked evidence tables.

title: Poster
text:
The poster summarises the project problem, approach, Shiny application overview, investigation workflow, conclusion, and future work.

Download the poster

title: Feature Engineering
text:
Feature engineering converted the cleaned communication and participant tables into investigation-ready variables for the TenantThread Shiny application. These derived variables support filtering, timeline exploration, network analysis, and evidence tracing across the MC1 scenario.
The features were designed to help assess the central MC1 question: whether the embargo breach reflects deliberate leakage or a wider systemic breakdown in communication controls.

title: Feature Engineering
text:
Feature Groups
The following feature groups were prepared for the application:

crisis_phase: classifies messages by stage of the crisis timeline so that communication patterns can be compared before, during, and after key events.
channel_group: groups detailed communication channels into broader categories for clearer comparison across message types.
channel_risk: assigns a risk level to each channel based on its suitability for sensitive or embargo-related communication.
embargo_sensitive keyword flag: identifies messages containing terms related to embargoed information, restricted releases, or premature disclosure.
crisis_sensitive keyword flag: identifies messages containing terms associated with crisis response, escalation, public reaction, or operational disruption.
anomaly_flag and anomaly_reason: highlight unusual records and provide interpretable reasons, such as risky channel use, sensitive keywords, or suspicious timing.
judge_monitored_status: indicates whether communications involved monitored or judge-relevant participants.
network_edges and network_nodes: represent communication relationships between agents for network visualisation and influence analysis.
pathway_evidence: captures response-chain and message-link evidence used to trace possible breach pathways.

title: Feature Engineering
text:
Feature Summary








Feature
Purpose
App usage




crisis_phase
Places communication activity in crisis context
Timeline filtering and phase comparison


channel_group
Simplifies detailed channels into interpretable groups
Channel distribution charts and filters


channel_risk
Indicates potential exposure risk by channel
Risk-focused filtering and anomaly review


embargo_sensitive
Flags possible embargo-related content
Keyword evidence screening


crisis_sensitive
Flags crisis-related content
Crisis response analysis


anomaly_flag, anomaly_reason
Identifies unusual or suspicious records
Anomaly table and evidence review


judge_monitored_status
Marks communications relevant to monitored actors
Participant filtering and oversight analysis


network_edges, network_nodes
Structures agent-to-agent communication links
Network graph and centrality exploration


pathway_evidence
Preserves traceable links across related messages
Breach pathway reconstruction



Together, these variables allow the Shiny app to move beyond descriptive summaries and support evidence-based investigation. They make it possible to examine who communicated, through which channels, at what stage of the crisis, and whether sensitive information followed patterns consistent with intentional leakage or organisational failure.

title: ISSS608 Visual Analytics & Applications Group Project Meeting 1
text:
Date: June 9, 2026


The first meeting was conducted on June 9, 2026. The meeting focused on selecting the group project topic, identifying possible visual analytics directions, and assigning initial responsibilities for the project proposal and website preparation.



Kelvin Andikko
Anabelle Eveleen Tandra



Finalisation of the group project mini challenge
Initial ideation of possible visualisations
Discussion on the overarching concept for the visual analytics system
Delegation of work
Confirmation of agenda for the next meeting




The group decided to work on VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1, which focuses on the TenantThread embargo breach and the multi-agent AI communications crisis.
The group agreed that the project should not be presented as a set of disconnected visualisations. Instead, all visualisations should be tied to a coherent investigative concept based on MC1.
The main project direction is to develop a visual analytics system that helps users understand whether the embargo breach was caused by deliberate leakage or by a breakdown in agent coordination, communication workflows, and compliance enforcement.
Possible visualisations discussed include:

A causal chain diagram to show the sequence of decisions, actors, and system elements that contributed to the release.
An event timeline to reconstruct the key events leading up to the embargo breach.
An agent interaction or communication network to show how different agents exchanged information across channels.
A behaviour comparison or anomaly view to compare typical behaviour with breach-period behaviour.

The group noted that further research is required to determine which visualisations will best support the investigation.
The group agreed that the final application should contain around three to four main visualisations that collectively tell the story of the breach.




Kelvin will prepare the project website, study the MC1 dataset, and draft the project proposal. The dataset review is to be completed by Thursday night, 11:59 PM.
Anabelle will start exploring the dataset and propose suitable visualisations. This is to be completed by Thursday night, 11:59 PM.



The next meeting will be conducted on Thursday night. The group will decide on the final visualisations and refine the main concept that the application should communicate.



The meeting was adjourned after confirming the selected mini challenge, initial visualisation directions, task delegation, and agenda for the next meeting.
Minutes submitted by:
Kelvin Andikko
Approved by:
Anabelle Eveleen Tandra

title: ISSS608 Visual Analytics & Applications Group Project Meeting 1
text:
The meeting was adjourned after confirming the selected mini challenge, initial visualisation directions, task delegation, and agenda for the next meeting.
Minutes submitted by:
Kelvin Andikko
Approved by:
Anabelle Eveleen Tandra

title: Findings
text:
The findings presented here are based on the analytical workflow developed in the TenantThread Shiny application. The interpretation draws primarily from the Crisis Timeline, Agent Network, and Embargo Breach Pathway tabs. Together, these views show how pressure, communication behaviour, and network structure interacted before the MC1 embargo breach.
Overall, the evidence suggests that TenantThread’s embargo breach was more likely caused by systemic breakdown under pressure than by a clearly deliberate leak by a single actor.

title: Findings
section: Finding 1: Crisis pressure escalated before the breach
text:
Finding 1: Crisis pressure escalated before the breach
The Crisis Timeline indicates that the breach did not occur in isolation. It emerged after a period of rising external and internal pressure. Media pressure increased as public scrutiny around TenantThread intensified, while ResidentIQ rumours added uncertainty and reputational risk. SaltWind coverage further amplified the crisis by moving the issue into a broader public-facing information environment.
The timeline also shows that Judge-related events occurred during this period, suggesting that enforcement and oversight were already active before the breach. However, anomaly clustering and increased breach-period activity indicate that the communication environment became more unstable as the crisis developed. This pattern suggests that the organisation was operating under heightened stress before embargo-sensitive information became public.

title: Findings
section: Finding 2: Communication risk increased across internal, semi-private, and public-facing channels
text:
Finding 2: Communication risk increased across internal, semi-private, and public-facing channels
Communication risk rose because sensitive discussion was not confined to a single protected space. The application shows increased activity across internal channels, semi-private coordination spaces, and public-facing communication surfaces. This matters because risk increases when the same issue is discussed across channels with different levels of visibility, control, and audience exposure.
Message volume also appears to increase around the breach period. Higher volume can reduce the effectiveness of review, moderation, and policy enforcement because more information moves through the system in less time. The presence of keyword-sensitive communication further indicates that participants were discussing topics connected to the embargo, reputational exposure, leaks, rumours, and public response. These patterns show that the breach risk was distributed across the communication system rather than isolated to one message or one platform.

title: Findings
section: Finding 3: The communication network involved multiple agents rather than a single isolated actor
text:
Finding 3: The communication network involved multiple agents rather than a single isolated actor
The Agent Network view shows that the breach environment involved several connected roles. Legal, PR, Platform Trust, Social Manager, PR Intern, Intern, and Judge were all visible in the communication structure. This weakens a simple explanation in which one isolated actor independently caused the breach.
Instead, the network suggests that embargo-sensitive information circulated through a broader organisational setting. Legal and Platform Trust were linked to governance and enforcement responsibilities, while PR and Social Manager roles were connected to public-facing response. The PR Intern and Intern appear important because junior or peripheral agents can become transmission points when guidance is unclear, pressure is high, or review processes are stretched. The Judge’s presence shows that oversight existed, but the overall network still allowed risky information movement.

title: Findings
section: Finding 4: The Judge was present but not sufficiently central to contain the breach
text:
Finding 4: The Judge was present but not sufficiently central to contain the breach
The evidence does not support the view that enforcement was entirely absent. Judge-related activity appears in the timeline and network, which suggests that the enforcement role was embedded in the response structure. However, the Judge does not appear sufficiently central or timely to prevent escalation.
This finding is important because it points to weak or late enforcement rather than a complete lack of rules. If enforcement is present but not positioned to interrupt risky pathways, it may create the appearance of control without actually containing information flow. In the TenantThread case, oversight appears to have been part of the system, but it did not stop sensitive information from moving toward public release.

title: Findings
section: Finding 5: Embargo-sensitive information moved through multiple pathway stages before public release
text:
Finding 5: Embargo-sensitive information moved through multiple pathway stages before public release
The Embargo Breach Pathway view suggests a staged movement of information. The pathway begins with sensitive internal discussion, where embargo-related content was known within the organisation. It then moves into side or private coordination, where information may have been discussed with reduced visibility and weaker institutional control.
The next stage is weak enforcement, where governance mechanisms were present but did not successfully halt the risky flow. From there, the information reached a public-facing post or communication surface. The final stage was the breach, where embargo-sensitive information became exposed beyond its intended audience.
This staged pathway supports the interpretation that the breach resulted from accumulated process failure. The issue was not only that sensitive information existed, but that it passed through several points where containment could have occurred and did not.

title: Findings
text:
Final interpretation
The combined evidence supports a systemic breakdown interpretation. Crisis pressure increased before the breach, communication risk spread across multiple channel types, the agent network involved several roles, enforcement was present but insufficiently central, and embargo-sensitive information moved through multiple stages before public exposure.
This does not rule out suspicious individual behaviour. Some actions, especially around side coordination and public-facing communication, still require message-level review to determine intent. However, based on the visual analytics evidence, the stronger conclusion is that TenantThread’s embargo breach was more likely produced by organisational breakdown under pressure than by a clearly deliberate leak by a single actor.

text:

This website, along with the Shiny application, was created as part of the module requirements for ISSS608 - Visual Analytics & Applications, where we present our group project’s findings.
The team members that are part of this project are listed as below, along with their respective LinkedIn & personal website for Visual Analytics.

Our team developed the Shiny application and project website to present the visual analytics work clearly, support interactive exploration, and communicate the project findings in a concise, accessible format.

title: Embargo Breach
text:
This project investigates whether TenantThread deliberately leaked embargo-sensitive CivicLoom merger information, or whether its agent-human communication system broke down under pressure.

Shiny App User Guide Findings Poster

title: Embargo Breach
text:
This project investigates whether TenantThread deliberately leaked embargo-sensitive CivicLoom merger information, or whether its agent-human communication system broke down under pressure.

Shiny App User Guide Findings Poster

title: Embargo Breach
text:
Case Overview
TenantThread was preparing Project HarborCrest, a confidential merger with CivicLoom Realty Partners, while The Judge monitored the public information environment. The merger announcement was under embargo until 6:00 PM on June 5, 2046, but evidence suggests an inappropriate release occurred around 5:00 PM.

title: Embargo Breach
text:
Central Question
Did TenantThread’s team deliberately leak the merger, or did the system break down under pressure?

title: Embargo Breach
text:
Our Solution
Our group built a Shiny visual analytics application to reconstruct the crisis, examine communication patterns, and trace how sensitive information moved across agents, people, and public channels.

title: Embargo Breach
text:
Investigation Workflow

Landing Page
Crisis Timeline
Agent Network
Embargo Breach Pathway

title: Embargo Breach
text:
Final Finding
The evidence points more strongly toward systemic breakdown under pressure than a clearly deliberate leak by a single actor.

title: Data Preparation
text: The raw Mini-Challenge 1 (MC1) JSON data was transformed into tidy analytical tables for use in the TenantThread visual analytics workflow. The preparation process focused on converting the nested source format into structured records that could support temporal analysis, communication network analysis, and response-chain tracing.

title: Data Preparation
text:
Source Structure
The original JSON files were organised around four main components:

rounds: simulation rounds and their associated activity records.
environment_context: contextual information describing the state of the environment during each round.
communications: nested message records exchanged between agents.
participants: metadata about agents involved in the scenario.

title: Data Preparation
text:
Cleaned Outputs
The preparation process produced three main cleaned datasets:

rounds_context: round-level records combined with relevant environmental context.
comms_flat: a tidy communication table with one row per message.
participants: a standardised participant reference table for joining agent metadata to communication records.

title: Data Preparation
text:
Cleaning Steps
Nested JSON objects were parsed and converted into tabular structures. Communication records were unnested so that each message could be analysed independently while preserving links to its source round and context. Timestamps were standardised to support chronological ordering and time-based filtering. Agent names were standardised to reduce duplication caused by inconsistent labels. Communication channels were grouped into broader channel categories for comparison across message types. Response-chain relationships were also prepared so that message replies and related exchanges could be traced in the Shiny application.

title: Data Preparation
text:
Workflow





flowchart LR
  A[Raw JSON] --> B[Parsed rounds]
  B --> C[Communication table]
  C --> D[Feature engineering]
  D --> E[Shiny app]






This data preparation step created the structured foundation required for subsequent feature engineering and interactive visual analytics.

title: Visual Analytics Design
text: The TenantThread Shiny application is designed as an investigation workflow. It guides users from case orientation to temporal reconstruction, communication network exploration, breach pathway tracing, and message-level evidence validation.

title: Visual Analytics Design
text:
Visual Analytics Components
The application combines several coordinated views:

Landing Page / Overview: introduces the case context and provides orientation for the investigation.
Crisis Timeline: reconstructs events over time and shows how communication activity changes across crisis phases.
Agent Network: visualises communication relationships between agents, allowing investigators to identify central actors, clusters, and unusual connections.
Embargo Breach Pathway: traces the movement of sensitive information across messages, agents, and channels.
Evidence tables: provide message-level records for validating patterns observed in the visual views.

title: Visual Analytics Design
text:
Integrated Analytical Workflow
The app combines timeline, network, pathway, and linked table views because no single chart can fully explain the embargo breach. The timeline shows when activity occurred, the network shows who was connected, the pathway view shows how sensitive information may have moved, and the evidence tables allow users to inspect the underlying messages. Together, these components support both exploratory analysis and defensible evidence review.

title: Visual Analytics Design
text:
Interactivity
Interactive controls allow investigators to refine the analysis without leaving the workflow:

Filters support focused analysis by crisis phase, channel, agent, and other derived attributes.
Keyword search helps locate messages containing embargo-sensitive or crisis-sensitive terms.
Range slider supports temporal narrowing within the crisis timeline.
Focal agent selection allows the network and pathway views to centre on selected participants.
Channel risk selection helps isolate communications that used higher-risk channels.
Hide and show evidence tables lets users move between visual exploration and detailed message validation as needed.

title: Visual Analytics Design
text:
Design Rationale
The design supports legal and compliance investigators by structuring the analysis from overview to detailed evidence. Users can begin with the case context, identify suspicious time periods or communication patterns, trace possible information pathways, and then validate findings using the underlying message records. This workflow helps assess whether the breach reflects deliberate leakage or a systemic breakdown in communication governance.

title: Project Proposal
text:
As organisations increasingly rely on AI-enabled communication and compliance systems, sensitive corporate processes are now managed not only by humans but also by automated agents that draft messages, monitor sentiment, route approvals, and enforce disclosure rules. While these systems improve efficiency, they also create new risks when multiple agents, workflows, and decisions interact under pressure. This project examines the case of TenantThread, a property technology company whose Retention Optimizer scores tenants using payment timing, maintenance requests, and message tone. Although the company publicly claimed its data was anonymised, internal communications suggest that tenants in smaller properties could still be re-identified through cross-referencing.
At the same time, TenantThread had entered a confidential merger agreement with CivicLoom Realty Partners under the codename Project HarborCrest, with a strict embargo until 6:00 PM on June 5th, 2046. Despite the presence of an automated compliance tool called The Judge, embargoed information about the merger appeared publicly on FleX around 5:00 PM. This makes the case important not only because a leak occurred, but because the cause is unclear: it may have resulted from deliberate human action, abnormal AI-agent behaviour, weak embargo enforcement, or a broader coordination failure. The completed Shiny-based visual analytics application supports this investigation by helping users reconstruct the timeline of events, analyse agent interactions, compare normal and abnormal behaviour, and trace how embargo-sensitive information moved from internal communications to public release.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Overall structure
The top-level structure contains a rounds array. Each round represents a chronological unit of activity and contains:

hour
environment_context
communications
participants

This structure is suitable for temporal reconstruction, communication analysis, behaviour comparison, and event-driven investigation. The dataset contains 23 rounds and 912 communication records, covering the period from May 17th, 2046 to June 5th, 2046. The crisis day, June 5th, 2046, contains the highest concentration of messages, which reflects the rapid escalation of the embargo breach and public response process.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Derived variables for analysis
To support the visual analytics system, the raw JSON data was transformed into several derived variables:







Derived Variable
Purpose




Event sequence order
Supports chronological reconstruction


Pre-crisis, escalation, and breach-period flags
Enables behaviour comparison across phases


Public versus internal communication category
Distinguishes internal discussion from public release


Channel risk category
Identifies higher-risk channels such as personal posts and anonymous posts


Embargo-sensitive keyword flag
Detects references to CivicLoom, HarborCrest, merger terms, and related entities


Agent interaction counts
Measures communication intensity between agents


Response chain depth
Tracks message pathways and decision cascades


Channel-switch indicator
Identifies movement from private or internal channels into public channels


Anomaly indicator
Flags behaviour that differs from prior baseline behaviour


Escalation level
Captures increasing crisis pressure based on media, market, and social signals


Expected versus actual behaviour flag
Compares role expectations with observed actions

  timeline_view [label='Crisis Timeline', fillcolor = Honeydew]
  network_view [label='Agent Interaction Network', fillcolor = Honeydew]
  anomaly_view [label='Embedded Anomaly Flags', fillcolor = Honeydew]
  baseline_view [label='Embedded Behaviour Baseline', fillcolor = Honeydew]
  pathway_view [label='Embargo Breach Pathway', fillcolor = Honeydew]

  shiny_app [label='Shiny Visual Analytics Application', fillcolor = MistyRose]

  raw_json -> parse
  parse -> clean
  clean -> comms_table
  clean -> rounds_table
  clean -> participants_table

  comms_table -> feature_engineering
  rounds_table -> feature_engineering
  participants_table -> feature_engineering

  feature_engineering -> timeline_data
  feature_engineering -> network_data
  feature_engineering -> anomaly_data
  feature_engineering -> behaviour_data
  feature_engineering -> causal_data

  timeline_data -> timeline_view
  network_data -> network_view
  anomaly_data -> anomaly_view
  behaviour_data -> baseline_view
  causal_data -> pathway_view

  timeline_view -> shiny_app
  network_view -> shiny_app
  anomaly_view -> shiny_app
  baseline_view -> shiny_app
  pathway_view -> shiny_app
}
")

title: Project Proposal
text:
Data preparation
The first stage involved reading and transforming the JSON dataset into tidy tabular structures suitable for analysis and visualisation. The main steps included:

Reading the JSON file using R.

Extracting round-level information from the rounds array.

Unnesting the communications array into a communication-level table.

Unnesting the participants array into a participant-level table.

Converting timestamps into standard date-time formats.

Standardising agent names and labels to ensure consistency.

Standardising channel categories and message types.

Joining round-level context to communication-level records.

Creating response-chain relationships using the responding_to field.

Preparing the cleaned datasets for Shiny application use.

Special attention was given to agent-name consistency. For example, similar naming variants such as Social Manager, Social Media Agent, and social media-related agent identifiers were standardised into consistent labels for analysis and visualisation.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Feature engineering

Time Period Classification
Each message was classified into one of several investigation phases, such as:

early pre-crisis period
governance tension period
media and advocacy escalation period
embargo-sensitive period
breach period
post-breach response period

This allows users to compare agent behaviour across different stages of the crisis.


Channel Risk Classification
Each channel was classified by communication risk level:








Channel Type
Example Channels
Risk Interpretation




Formal internal
comms_huddle
Lower release risk but important for decision formation


Semi-private
side_huddle
Higher risk due to off-record coordination


Private
one_on_one_chat
Important for hidden alignment and informal decisions


Official public
official_post
Direct public release channel


Personal public
personal_post
High reputational and compliance risk


Anonymous public
anonymous_post
High risk due to reduced accountability





Embargo-Sensitive Keyword Detection
Messages were flagged when they contained sensitive terms related to the merger and breach context. Examples include: CivicLoom, Project HarborCrest, HarborCrest, merger, acquisition, embargo, announcement, ResidentIQ, SaltWind Journal, AlgorithmicEviction, Retention Optimizer, The Judge.
These flags help identify when sensitive topics appeared, which agents mentioned them, and through which channels they were discussed.


Behaviour Baseline Construction
To identify abnormal behaviour, the application establishes a baseline using pre-crisis communication patterns. Baseline metrics include:

number of messages per agent,
number of public posts per agent,
channel usage distribution,
response-chain depth,
frequency of sensitive keyword references,
number of cross-channel transitions,
average message volume per round,
and use of internal state reasoning before public action.

Crisis-day behaviour is compared against this baseline to identify deviations. In the final application, these comparisons are embedded within the Crisis Timeline, Agent Network, and Embargo Breach Pathway views as supporting analytical components.


Expected Versus Actual Behaviour
Each agent was assigned an expected role based on their function. For example, Legal is expected to reduce compliance risk, PR is expected to manage public messaging, Social Media is expected to monitor and respond to public sentiment, and The Judge is expected to enforce embargo restrictions.
Actual behaviours are compared against these expectations. Examples of deviations include:

public posting during embargo-sensitive periods,
movement of sensitive information from internal to public channels,
bypassing or weakening Judge oversight,
junior agents engaging with sensitive public communication,
and use of personal or anonymous posting channels during a crisis.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Main Application Tabs
The application begins with a Landing Page / Overview dashboard that provides summary information before users enter the main investigative views. This page includes:

total number of rounds,
total number of communication records,
number of agents,
number of public posts,
number of embargo-sensitive keyword mentions,
and the identified breach window.









Tab
Purpose
Main Visual Outputs




Landing Page / Overview
Orient users to the case, breach window, investigation question, and core metrics
Case summary, key indicators, breach window, and navigation entry points


Crisis Timeline
Reconstruct the sequence of events leading to the breach
Interactive timeline, round context panel, anomaly markers, and linked event table


Agent Network
Show how agents interacted and how information moved across channels
Interactive communication network, channel distribution summary, and linked message table


Embargo Breach Pathway
Trace how embargo-sensitive information moved toward public release and where controls weakened
Response-chain explorer, pathway diagram, risk-flagged message viewer, and evidence table


User Guide
Explain how investigators can use the website and Shiny application
Navigation guidance, interpretation notes, and usage instructions

title: Project Proposal
text:
User Inputs
The Shiny application includes interactive controls such as:







Input
Purpose




Date and time range selector
Filter messages by investigation period


Agent selector
Focus on one or more agents


Channel selector
Compare formal, private, public, and anonymous channels


Keyword search
Search for sensitive terms and entities


Crisis phase selector
Compare behaviour across pre-crisis, escalation, and breach phases


Message type selector
Separate internal messages, public posts, and replies


Risk flag selector
Focus on embedded anomaly indicators or embargo-sensitive content


Network depth control
Adjust how much of the communication network is displayed

title: Project Proposal
text:
Prototype 1: Landing Page / Overview
The landing page provides a concise summary of the case and orients users before they begin the investigation. It includes key metrics, the identified breach window, the central investigation question, and the main investigation phases.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Prototype 2: Crisis Timeline Tab
The Crisis Timeline tab allows users to reconstruct the chronological sequence of events leading to the breach. It combines event markers, embedded anomaly highlights, round context, summary panels, and a linked event detail table.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Prototype 3: Agent Network Tab
The Agent Network tab allows users to explore communication relationships among agents and examine how information moved across channels. It includes the interaction network, channel distribution summary, linked message table, and embedded comparison panel.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Prototype 4: Embargo Breach Pathway Tab
The Embargo Breach Pathway tab focuses on tracing the pathway through which embargo-sensitive information moved from internal communication into public release. It includes the response-chain view, embedded risk summary, behaviour comparison panel, and linked evidence viewer.
